Chachmei Lublin Yeshiva.event: Founded by Rabbi Meir Shapiro in Lublin, Poland.

Kamenitz Yeshiva.event: Founded in Kamenets-Litovsk by Rabbi Baruch Ber Leibowitz.
Became known as a premier Lithuanian yeshiva.

Kelm Talmud Torah Yeshiva.event: Rabbi Simcha Zissel Ziv appointed head and transformed the Talmud Torah into a Musar-oriented yeshiva.
Permanent campus purchased and new building constructed.
Exiled to Grobin, Courland (now Latvia), where Rabbi Ziv reestablished the yeshiva temporarily.
Returned to Kelm; developed into a central Musar yeshiva.
Death of Rabbi Simcha Zissel Ziv; succession by his brother Rabbi Aryeh Leib Broida, later his son-in-law Rabbi Tzvi Hirsch Broida and Rabbi Nachum Ze’ev Ziv.
Leadership passed to Rabbi Reuven Dov Dessler and then to Rabbis Daniel Movshovitz and Gershon Miadnik.
On June 21, 1941, Lithuanian collaborators and the Gestapo murdered the students and townspeople; the yeshiva was destroyed.

Mir Yeshiva.event: World War II outbreak; yeshiva fled eastward.
Entire yeshiva arrived in Shanghai, China, where it remained through the war.
Reestablished in Jerusalem by Rabbi Eliezer Yehuda Finkel.
Branch established in Brooklyn, New York.

Novardok Yeshiva.event: Founded by Rabbi Yosef Yozel Horwitz.
The size of the Yeshivah reaches 200 pupils.
After the Bolshevik takeover of Russia, the Alter sends his students across to Poland in secrecy. Many were shot or sent to Siberia for doing so. 600 made it.
THe Yeshiva goes to Kiev fleeing the war. There is a typhus outbreak where the Alter passes away.

Radun Yeshiva.event: Founded in Radun, Belarus by the Chofetz Chaim.
Disrupted during World War I; students scattered.
Yeshiva rebuilt after the war; continued under Rabbi Naftoli Trop.
Destroyed during the Holocaust; students and faculty murdered.

Slabodka Yeshiva.event: Founded in Slabodka, Lithuania, under Rabbi Nosson Tzvi Finkel.
Branch established in Hebron, Mandatory Palestine.
Hebron massacre; yeshiva moved temporarily to Jerusalem.
Lithuanian yeshiva destroyed in the Holocaust.
Hebron branch reestablished permanently in Bnei Brak, Israel.

Mesivta Rabsa Eitz Chaim D'Slutsk (Sluts.event: Founded in Slutsk by Rabbi Yaakov Dovid Willovsky (Ridvaz), who invited top students from Slabodka Yeshiva; Rabbi Isser Zalman Meltzer appointed rosh yeshiva.
Rabbi Aharon Kotler led 50 students to escape Soviet Russia and reestablish the yeshiva in Kletsk, Poland.
New yeshiva building dedicated in Kletsk with thousands attending; Rabbi Meltzer traveled from Palestine to participate.
Yeshiva escaped to Lithuania; regrouped in Jonava near Kaunas under Rabbi Aharon Kotler, with ~150 students.
In 1940, the Soviets annexed Lithuania, and afraid, the yeshiva divided into three groups (to make it harder to destroy the entire yeshiva).

Telz Yeshiva.event: Flourished under Rabbi Eliezer Gordon and Rabbi Yosef Leib Bloch.
Reestablished in Cleveland, Ohio by survivors.
